channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Analysis of Satellite Image and Videos Suggest Precision U.S. Strikes on Iranian Water Facility
item.description: It is unclear if the U.S. intentionally struck the facility or knew what it was. Deliberately targeting civilian infrastructure could constitute a war crime.
item.dc:creator: Christoph Koettl and Christiaan Triebert
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity

item.title: Oil Prices Rise as U.S. Launches New Attack on Iran
item.description: Oil prices jumped after the United States and Iran exchanged fire for a second day, heightening fears that the two countries could slide back into open conflict.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump’s ‘Secret Mission’ to Ferry Oil Past Iran Was Widely Disclosed
item.description: A U.S. military official said the president’s seemingly dramatic announcement on Wednesday referred to a previously reported effort to shepherd commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Jonathan Swan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, seen from Oman on Wednesday.

item.title: FISA Surveillance Law May Expire After Trump Picks Bill Pulte for Intel Post
item.description: Republicans are struggling to extend a powerful surveillance authority set to lapse this weekend after President Trump alienated lawmakers with his choice of acting spy chief.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz and Robert Jimison
item.category: Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:description: President Trump last week named Bill Pulte, a confidant without any national security experience, as acting director of national intelligence.

item.title: Will Trump’s Repairs to Washington D.C.’s Reflecting Pool Work?
item.description: President Trump has said that construction is complete. Now comes the real test. Will the longstanding problems finally be solved?
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Shrugs Off High Prices Amid Iran War and Says ‘I Love the Inflation’
item.description: As inflation outpaces workers’ wages, the Trump administration insists that its agenda is working.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Speaking at a bill signing on Wednesday, President Trump predicted that inflation would recede quickly, once the Iran conflict concludes.“ It’s going to come down like a rock.”

item.title: Trump Muses About Government Taking a Piece of A.I. Companies
item.description: President Trump touched on what is an increasingly hot topic in Washington: how average Americans can get a piece of the tech industry’s A.I. windfall.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle, Ana Swanson and Cecilia Kang
item.media:description: President Trump, shown here in the Oval Office on Wednesday, has not been shy about the government’s taking a stake in companies.

item.title: Mamdani’s Nosebleed Knicks Ticket and the Benefits of Being Mayor
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani paid roughly $1,000 for a standing-room-only ticket to Game 3 of the N.B.A. finals, using access to house tickets not available to regular fans.
item.dc:creator: Dana Rubinstein and Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani attended the Knicks’ Game 3 loss in the N.B.A. finals with some former colleagues in the State Legislature.

item.title: Victor Wembanyama, the Knicks’ Biggest Foe and New York’s Biggest Tourist
item.description: Mr. Wembanyama has had to keep a low-profile while in New York to try and win the N.B.A. finals, but on visits there, he has always resisted hiding in hotel rooms and private cars.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli
item.media:description: On visits to New York City, Victor Wembanyama has played chess in Washington Park, taken the subway and drawn in Gramercy Park.

item.title: The U.S. Strikes Iran Again
item.description: Also, inflation jumps for a third-straight month. Here’s the latest at the end of Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Enghelab Square in Tehran on Monday.

item.title: SpaceX’s IPO Could Turn 4,400 Employees Into Millionaires
item.description: While Elon Musk may soon become a trillionaire, his rocket company’s market debut is set to the change the lives of its current and former employees, too.
item.dc:creator: Kirsten Grind

item.title: Antonio Gracias, Elon Musk’s Friend, Is Set to Reap Billions From SpaceX’s IPO
item.description: Antonio Gracias, an investor who has built a $65 billion stake in the company, exemplifies the economic upside of being Mr. Musk’s ally.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Jim Vondruska for The New York Times
item.media:description: Antonio Gracias, left, and Elon Musk at a political event in Green Bay, Wis., last spring. Mr. Gracias and his private equity firm control a $65 billion stake in SpaceX.

item.title: Inside Trump’s White House, the Epstein Files Caused a Freakout
item.description: The president’s top advisers gathered in a series of Situation Room meetings as they struggled to contain a scandal engulfing Donald Trump himself.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan

item.title: The White House Panic Over the Epstein Files: Six Takeaways From the New York Times Investigation
item.description: Senior officials clashed in a series of meetings as they struggled to manage a crisis over the president’s refusal to release the documents.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.media:description: Protesters at the White House last July.

item.title: Bill Gates Says Epstein Tried to Use His Extramarital Affairs Against Him
item.description: The billionaire philanthropist testified on Wednesday in a closed-door congressional hearing about the Justice Department’s investigation of Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Goldstein
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: Revelations about Bill Gates’s connection to Jeffrey Epstein came to light after Mr. Epstein was arrested in 2019.

item.title: The Big Questions About Jeffrey Epstein: What The Times Has Learned
item.description: Here is what we know about the sexual predator whose secrets spurred an international reckoning over money, power and complicity. Check back for updates.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Top Pentagon Official Worked Closely With C.I.A. Officer Later Found With Gold Bars
item.description: David Rush, the C.I.A. employee, worked on a highly classified China spying program with Stephen A. Feinberg, the Pentagon’s second-ranking official.
item.dc:creator: Mark Mazzetti and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Stephen A. Feinberg in February 2025, during his confirmation hearing to be deputy defense secretary.

item.title: Mace Defeat Heralds an Exodus of Rabble-Rousers From Congress
item.description: The Republican congresswoman from South Carolina, who made enemies on both sides of the political aisle, is just one of several flashy lawmakers to be leaving Capitol Hill.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Nancy Mace, Republican of South Carolina, at the Capitol earlier this year.

item.title: Democrats Try to Move Past ‘Cultural Pandering’ to Latinos for Midterm Elections
item.description: The party is trying desperately to win back some voters in the key swing group.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times

item.title: 7 Revolutionary-Era Bars That Are as Old as America
item.description: Taverns were important incubators of revolutionary fervor where patriots gathered to plot and gossip. Here are a few where you can still taste the spirit of 1776.
item.dc:creator: Liza Weisstuch and James Estrin

item.title: They Tried To Catch a Child Predator on a Livestream. They Trapped Themselves Instead.
item.description: What happened when livestreaming vigilantes ambushed an innocent man.
item.dc:creator: Kashmir Hill and Aric Toler

item.title: Australia’s Social Media Ban Is Floundering. Can It Still Help Younger Kids?
item.description: Six months in, many teens are already back on platforms they were supposed to be blocked from. The ban’s benefits may fall to the next generation.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.media:credit: Adam Ferguson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The mother of Jimmy, 12, said she tried to build relationships with families that share her concerns about social media and to nurture his interests in activities that don’t involve screens.

item.title: Britain Is Weighing a Social Media Ban for Children. How Did It Get Here?
item.description: Months after Australia banned social media for everyone under 16, the British government is considering new policies to keep children safe online.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Using a smartphone in Sydney, Australia, last year. In December, the country banned social media for those under 16.

item.title: Canada Moves to Ban Social Media Use for Youth Under 16
item.description: The country’s previous attempt to get tech companies to shelter young users failed amid heavy criticism from civil liberty groups.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:description: People holding up cellphones at a concert in Toronto.

item.title: FIFA President Tells World Cup Critics to ‘Chill’ About Concerns
item.description: With the World Cup facing growing crises, Gianni Infantino, the FIFA president, struck a defiant tone on the eve of the competition’s start.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: FIFA is trying its best, Gianni Infantino said during a news conference on Wednesday, but “we don’t control everything.”

item.title: Amtrak Speeds Up Trip From Vancouver to Seattle for World Cup Fans
item.description: Passengers heading from Vancouver, British Columbia, to Seattle will now clear U.S. customs before boarding instead of stopping once they’ve crossed the border.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:description: Amtrak’s Cascades line takes about four hours to get from Vancouver, British Columbia, which is hosting seven World Cup matches, to Seattle, which is hosting six.

item.title: A ‘Sultry’ Shift: Heat Creeps Into the Northeast
item.description: Soupy, hot weather will settle into the Northeast on Thursday and Friday, bringing with it the threat of dangerous storms.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: A security guard in New York cooling down during a hot day last summer. This week will bring the year’s first widespread dose of summerlike heat and humidity to the city.

item.title: Storms Knock Out Power for Hundreds of Thousands in the Midwest
item.description: Forecasters warned that destructive storms were expected across the region into Wednesday night.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar

item.title: Unusually Warm Weather in Bay Area Prompts Heat Alert and Wildfire Concerns
item.description: High temperatures across the San Francisco Bay Area will reach into the 90s on Thursday, forecasters said.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.media:credit: Lea Suzuki/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Some parts of the Bay Area could reach close to 100 degrees on Thursday, the National Weather Service said.

item.title: Spencer Pratt’s Defeat Isn’t Just About Los Angeles
item.description: Some of America’s largest cities are now being governed by progressives. What will we learn?
item.dc:creator: David Wallace-Wells

item.title: Republicans Thought They Owned ‘Values.’ Not Anymore.
item.description: A new politics of morality is rising in the Democratic Party.
item.dc:creator: E.J. Dionne Jr.

item.title: Is A.I. affecting your career? We want to hear from you.
item.description: The New York Times wants to talk to workers about what they think artificial intelligence will mean for their careers, and how they are navigating this uncertain period.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman

item.title: Nearly Everyone, Everywhere, Veers Left When Walking
item.description: Researchers are at a loss for why people across cultures and ages, regardless of their dominant hand, have a natural bias toward wandering in a counterclockwise direction.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer
